data:
9th Grade. The entry point into full participation in the life of the church.
Let’s be very clear about what Confirmation is — and is not:
It is not graduation from church. It is not a gold star for attending. It is not a hoop to jump through so parents get the photo. It is three things, all at once:
God confirms a youth’s baptismal identity as a beloved child of God. The youth confirms their faith in Jesus Christ — not their parents’ faith, not their grandmother’s faith, their own. Everyone confirms that this is the beginning, not the end. Confirmation works best when it’s relational, not informational. A student will forget most of the doctrine by Thanksgiving. They won’t forget the adult who knew their name, asked their hard questions, and prayed for them by name on Confirmation Sunday.
data.series#Series: 🛠️ Still under construction. A full Confirmation curriculum is coming. In the meantime, if your church has a framework that works — the Apostles’ Creed, the Westminster Shorter Catechism, the Heidelberg, or something homegrown — please contribute it.
data.whats-coming#What\u0026rsquo;s coming:
Planned curriculum:
Who Is God? — The Trinity without the word salad Who Is Jesus? — Looking squarely at the claims, the cross, and the resurrection Who Am I? — Identity as a baptized child of God What Do We Do Now? — Worship, Word, sacraments, and service as a way of life The Credo Project — each student writes their own “I believe” statement to share on Confirmation Sunday

data:
Grades 9–12. The ages that ask the hardest questions, barter for compliance, and will spot spiritual BS at 400 yards. So we try not to serve any.
HS lessons on Opensource Sunday are built for students who are done being entertained and ready to be engaged. The template is the same as Middle School — Behind the Curtain → Center Stage → Prop Department → Directing the Scene — but with heavier discussion prompts, more Scripture per session, and fewer snack-based metaphors (though the snacks themselves remain non-negotiable).
data.series#Series: 🚧 Nothing to show here yet. The HS curriculum is still being drafted. If you’ve got a series you want to contribute — or a lesson you’ve already taught that killed — open a PR.
data.whats-coming#What\u0026rsquo;s coming:
Planned series include:
Romans Road Trip — an 8-week walk through Paul’s letter to Rome Hard Sayings of Jesus — 6 weeks on the verses nobody puts on a coffee mug The Psalms, for People Who Aren’t Fine — 5 weeks in the raw honesty of the Psalms Apologetics Without the Argument — 4 weeks on doubt, evidence, and faith Stay tuned. Or, better yet, write one yourself.

data:
Grades 6–8. The ages most adults pretend they forgot. The ages that, according to the Barna Group, are when nearly 64% of lifelong Christians first made a commitment to Christ. Coincidence? Probably not.
These lessons are written for the real middle schooler — the one who laughs too loud, then goes quiet when it matters, then asks a question that’ll make you rethink your whole theology on the car ride home.
data.series#Series: Sermon on the MountNine weeks in Matthew 5–7. A new way to be human.
data.what-to-expect-from-an-ms-lesson#What to:
Every lesson on this site follows the same template so volunteers aren’t left guessing:
Behind the Curtain — what the teacher needs to know before walking in Center Stage — the ONE thing you want students to walk out with Prop Department — the shopping list Directing the Scene — the actual plan: prayer, hook, Scripture, discussion, and a tangible “turn toward action” All MS lessons target ~45–60 minutes and are written assuming a group of 6–15 students, family-style. Scale the activities to your room.

data: A nine-week series through the Sermon on the Mount. Scripture anchor: Matthew 5–7 Series ONE thing: Jesus doesn’t just want us to follow the rules harder — he’s inviting us into a brand new way to be human, built on him.
data.jump-to-a-lesson#Jump to a lesson: Lesson 1 · Up the MountainMatthew 5:1–12 · The Beatitudes — who gets called blessed? Lesson 2 · Salt and LightMatthew 5:13–16 · Flavor and illumination Lesson 3 · But I SayMatthew 5:17–48 · Heart, not just behavior draft Lesson 4 · When You PrayMatthew 6:5–15 · Talk to your Father draft Lesson 5 · Treasures & Two MastersMatthew 6:19–24 · What has your heart has your life draft Lesson 6 · Do Not WorryMatthew 6:25–34 · Trust the One who feeds sparrows draft Lesson 7 · Judge NotMatthew 7:1–6 · Check the plank before the speck draft Lesson 8 · Ask, Seek, KnockMatthew 7:7–12 · A good Father and the Golden Rule draft Lesson 9 · House on the RockMatthew 7:24–29 · Hear and do. Build on Jesus. draft Leading this series? The nine lessons are designed as one arc — identity first, then witness, heart, prayer, treasure, trust, judgment, asking, and finally foundation. Lesson 9 builds an explicit series recap into the teaching, so the ordering matters. If you only have time for a shorter arc, at minimum do Lessons 1 and 9 together.

data: Scripture: Matthew 5:1–12 Series: Sermon on the Mount
data.behind-the-curtain#Behind the Curtain:
What do I need to know before teaching?
By the end of Matthew 4, Jesus has become the most talked-about rabbi in the region. He’s been traveling all over Galilee, teaching in synagogues, proclaiming the good news of the kingdom, and healing every kind of sickness and pain. Crowds from Galilee, the Decapolis, Jerusalem, Judea, and beyond the Jordan are chasing him down. Then, in Matthew 5:1, he sees the crowd, walks up a mountainside, sits down (the posture of a teacher in that day), and begins the most famous sermon ever preached.
Before Jesus says a single word about how to live, he tells us who God is cheering for. The “Beatitudes” (from the Latin beatus — blessed, happy, flourishing) are not a checklist of behaviors to unlock God’s favor. They are Jesus announcing that God’s kingdom runs on an upside-down scoreboard. The people the world calls losers — the poor in spirit, the mourners, the meek, the hungry, the merciful, the pure in heart, the peacemakers, the persecuted — God calls blessed. That would have sounded as scandalous then as it does now.
For middle schoolers, this is enormous. They live in a world where “blessed” almost always means rich, pretty, followed, talented, winning. Jesus just took a sharpie to that whole list.
Two patterns worth pointing out:
Every Beatitude has the same shape: Blessed are ___, for they will ___. A condition, then a promise. The bookends match: verses 3 and 10 both end “for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.” Jesus wraps the whole list in the same promise — this isn’t a waiting game. The kingdom is already here for these people. This lesson is the first of two in the Sermon on the Mount series. Next week we fly through the rest of the sermon (Matt 5:13–7:27). Tonight we stay parked on the Beatitudes.
data.catch-their-attention--the-scoreboard#Ca:
Before the lesson: Write across the top of the whiteboard in big letters: “WHO IS BLESSED?”
During the lesson: Say:
Raise your hand if you’ve ever heard someone say “I’m blessed” — on a post, in a song, on a jersey, anywhere. When people say that, what do they usually mean?
Let students shout out answers. Write them on the board in one color. (You’ll probably get: rich, healthy, popular, good family, winning, famous, lots of followers, talented, pretty, etc.)
Then say:
Okay — same question, but now in Jesus’ version. If Jesus wrote the list, who do you think he’d put on it?
Don’t give the answer yet. Let them guess. Write their guesses in a different color. Then say:
Hold onto that. Let’s read what Jesus actually says — and see how close we got.
data.center-stage#Center Stage:
What are the most important takeaways?
God’s kingdom runs on an upside-down scoreboard. The people the world overlooks are the ones Jesus calls “blessed.”
data.directing-the-scene#Directing the Scene: What learning activities might we do?
data.ponder-the-possibilities#Ponder the Poss:
Discuss together:
Looking back at our board — how different is Jesus’ list from what the world calls “blessed”? Which of the eight Beatitudes hits you the hardest right now? Not the easiest. The one you need the most. Who in your school do most people ignore or laugh at — who Jesus might actually be calling “blessed”? Have you ever felt like one of the people on Jesus’ list? (Poor in spirit, grieving, small, overlooked?) What would it mean to you to hear Jesus call you “blessed” right there? Why do you think Jesus started his biggest sermon with this list? What is he trying to do to us before he teaches us anything else?
data.prop-department#Prop Department:
What supplies do I need?
Bibles, one per student Whiteboard or large paper + markers (two colors) Index cards — one per student Pens/pencils Christ light / candle + matches (for opening) Optional: a printed card for each student with all 8 Beatitudes to take home
data.read-and-interpret--matthew-5112#Read an:
Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:
Over the next two weeks we’re going to be in the most famous sermon Jesus ever preached — something called the Sermon on the Mount. Three whole chapters of Jesus teaching us how to live in God’s kingdom. Today we’re reading the very beginning — who showed up, where Jesus sat down, and the first words out of his mouth: eight blessings we call the Beatitudes.
Read Matthew 5:1–12 together. Go around the circle; each student reads one verse. Anyone who doesn’t want to read can say “pass” — no pressure.
Discuss:
Before Jesus even says a word, what kind of crowd is showing up? (look back at Matt 4:23–25) — Sick people. Hurting people. People from everywhere. Not the cool kids. Jesus uses the word “blessed” over and over. How is his idea of “blessed” different from the one on our board? Do you notice a pattern in how each Beatitude is worded? — Blessed are ___, for they will ___. A condition, then a promise. Look at verses 3 and 10. What do they have in common? — Same promise: theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Jesus bookends the whole list with it. The kingdom isn’t a someday thing — it’s here, already, for these people. Then walk through the eight blessings. Don’t lecture each one — ask, let them answer, fill in where needed.
“Poor in spirit” (v.3) — Spiritually empty-handed. Knowing you don’t have it all together. The opposite of proud. “Those who mourn” (v.4) — Why would Jesus bless grieving people? Because God is the God who comforts. “The meek” (v.5) — Meek isn’t weak. Meek is strength under control. Think a trained warhorse. Think Moses. Think Jesus. “Hunger and thirst for righteousness” (v.6) — A gut-level craving for what’s right. And the promise: they will be filled. “The merciful” (v.7) — Mercy isn’t just being nice. Mercy is giving someone what they don’t deserve — because God gave us what we didn’t deserve. “Pure in heart” (v.8) — Not “perfect.” Undivided. Same person online, at school, at home, at Crossroads. “Peacemakers” (v.9) — Not peace-keepers (who stay quiet to avoid a fight). Peace-makers step into conflict to heal it. That’s what God did for us. “Persecuted for righteousness” (vv.10–12) — Following Jesus sometimes costs something. Jesus says: rejoice — you’re in good company with the prophets. Say:
Here’s what Jesus is doing. He is flipping the world’s scoreboard upside down. The world cheers for people who have it all together. Jesus cheers for people who know they don’t. The world runs away from people who are grieving. Jesus runs toward them. The world worships the loudest person in the room. Jesus blesses the one who doesn’t need to be.
These eight blessings are going to change how we look at our school, our team, our lunch table — and our own hearts.
data.start-with-prayer#Start with Prayer: Light the Christ light. Invite a student to lead in prayer. Thank God for the gift of the group, and ask the Holy Spirit to open hearts to the words of Jesus.
data.turn-toward-action--name-one#Turn Toward:
Hand each student an index card. Say:
On this card, write the name of one person in your life who fits Jesus’ list more than the world’s list. You don’t have to show anyone. Just write the name.
Slip it in your pocket, your phone case, or your Bible. Your job this week: pray for that person every day. And do one thing — literally one thing — that tells them they matter. Say hi. Save them a seat. Text them. Invite them. Anything.
End with prayer:
Jesus, thank you that your blessing doesn’t depend on the scoreboard of the world. That your blessing is given to the meek, the mourning, the peacemakers, and the pour in spirit; because that is who we are. Give us your eyes this week for the people on our cards. Amen.

data: Scripture: Matthew 5:13–16 Series: Sermon on the Mount
data.behind-the-curtain#Behind the Curtain:
What do I need to know before teaching?
Jesus has just finished the Beatitudes. Without so much as a breath, he pivots from “blessed are you…” to “you are the salt of the earth… you are the light of the world.” Notice what he didn’t say. He didn’t say try to be salt. He didn’t say if you get your act together, you might someday be light. He said you are. It’s an identity statement. The question isn’t whether your students are salt and light; the question is what kind of salt and light they’re being.
A few things worth knowing:
Salt, in the ancient world, was essential for life. It preserved food before refrigeration. It flavored what would otherwise be bland. It was so valuable that Roman soldiers were sometimes paid in salt (hence salary). Salt that’s lost its saltiness — contaminated, diluted — is useless. Jesus is saying: if a follower of mine loses the thing that makes them distinct, what’s the point? Light, in the ancient world, wasn’t a flip of a switch. An oil lamp was essential gear. A city on a hillside at night was visible for miles. You don’t light a lamp just to hide it under a bowl — that’s absurd. Light has a purpose: to be seen. Verse 16 gives the reason: “let your light shine before others, that they may see your good deeds and glorify your Father in heaven.” Not so you get noticed. Not so you look holy. So God gets noticed. For middle schoolers, the quiet temptation is to keep faith private — something you do at church, not at the lunch table. Jesus is saying: that’s not an option. You are already salt. You are already light. The only question is whether the world gets to taste it and see it.
data.catch-their-attention--the-taste-test--t:
Before the lesson: Set out two identical bowls. Fill one with the unsalted version of the snack, fill the other with the salted version. Keep the difference secret.
During the lesson:
Part 1 — The taste test. Pass out the unsalted snack first. Say nothing. Let them chew. Wait for the reactions.
What are we tasting? Anything? Anyone want seconds?
Then pass out the salted version. Same food. Completely different experience.
What changed? One ingredient. That’s it.
Part 2 — The lamp. Turn off the overhead lights, or dim them. Hand a student a flashlight. Have them turn it on.
What’s this for?
Then take the flashlight and put it under the bowl.
How useful is it now?
Say:
Hold onto both of those. Tonight Jesus gives his followers two names — and they’re the two things we just messed with.
data.center-stage#Center Stage:
What are the most important takeaways?
You are salt and light. Your life is meant to make God visible and tasteable to the people around you — not so you get noticed, so God does.
data.directing-the-scene#Directing the Scene: What learning activities might we do?
data.ponder-the-possibilities#Ponder the Poss:
Discuss together:
Where in your life are you “hiding your light”? At school? At practice? Online? Around certain friends? What would “salty” friendship look like for you this week? Not preachy — actually flavored. Who in your life is genuinely salt and light? Someone whose life makes you want to know God more? Is there a place where your faith feels awkward or private? Why do you think that is? Jesus says we shine so others will glorify God. What’s the difference between making yourself look good and making God look good?
data.prop-department#Prop Department:
What supplies do I need?
Bibles, one per student A taste test: two small bowls of the same snack (plain popcorn, unsalted tortilla chips, plain pretzels) — one unsalted, one salted. Keep them hidden at first. A flashlight + a cereal-bowl-sized container to cover it with Whiteboard or large paper + markers Index cards — one per student Pens/pencils Christ light / candle + matches (for opening)
data.read-and-interpret--matthew-51316#Read a:
Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:
Last week we sat in the Beatitudes — Jesus cheering for the poor in spirit, the mourners, the meek. Without even stopping for breath, he pivots and says two of the most famous lines in the whole sermon. Listen for the pattern.
Read Matthew 5:13–16 together. Go around the circle; each student reads 1–2 verses.
Discuss:
Jesus doesn’t say “try to be” salt and light. He says “you ARE.” What’s different about that? — It’s identity, not an assignment. You’re already salt. You’re already light. The question is how. What does salt do? — Preserves. Flavors. Wakes up bland food. Wakes up bland anything. What does light do? — Reveals. Guides. Warms. You can’t hide from it. Jesus says salt that’s lost its saltiness is “no longer good for anything, except to be thrown out.” What does he mean? — If a follower of Jesus loses the thing that makes them distinct, there’s nothing there. “A town built on a hill cannot be hidden.” (v.14) What’s that like today? — You can’t miss a lit-up stadium at night. You can’t miss a stage in a dark auditorium. Light gets noticed. Look at verse 16. WHY do we let our light shine? — So people see our good deeds and give glory to our Father. Not to us. The whole point is to point people to God. Say:
Here’s the thing Jesus refuses to let us do: keep our faith private. He doesn’t say “be salty at church” or “light up for youth group.” He says “you are the salt of the earth” and “you are the light of the world.” Your whole week. Your whole school. Your whole table. Your whole team.
And the point — the whole point — isn’t to make you look like a good kid. It’s to make God look like the good God he actually is. When your life tastes different and shines different, people get curious about who’s behind it.
data.start-with-prayer#Start with Prayer: Light the Christ light. Invite a student to lead in prayer. Ask God that this group wouldn’t be Sunday-only Christians — that we’d be the kind of salt and light that changes the flavor of our school this week.
data.turn-toward-action--one-place-this-week#:
Hand each student an index card. Say:
Two questions. Write the answers on your card.
1. ONE place this week I can be salt and light on purpose. (Your lunch table. Your team bus. That one group chat. One specific class.)
2. ONE specific thing I’ll do there. (Say hi to the kid who’s alone. Thank a teacher on your way out. Refuse to join the roast. Invite someone to Crossroads.)
Keep the card in your pocket, bible, or phone case. Look at it on your way to school.
Close with prayer. Pray that this group wouldn’t just come to church — that this group would be church, all week, in the places we already spend our time.
